The web package has the highest concentration of bidirectional coupling pairs (4 pairs) while the sourcevision and llm-client packages have zero — the disparity maps directly to the presence or absence of a hub zone; eliminating web-viewer's hub role would reduce the web package's bidirectional pair count to near zero

The use zone (viewer-prd-interaction) acts as a structural bridge importing from both web-viewer (12) and web-dashboard-platform (5) — if the web-viewer ↔ web-dashboard-platform cycle were resolved, the use zone would become the primary remaining coupling bridge between them

All test zones (web-unit, rex-e2e, integration, e2e) have zero outbound coupling to other production zones, confirming that test isolation is uniformly enforced across the monorepo — this is a positive systemic pattern worth preserving

The three perfectly isolated zones (sourcevision-analysis-engine, landing-page, llm-client) are all either foundation-tier or single-package zones — isolation correlates with architectural tier, with hub and platform zones universally showing bidirectional coupling

The rex package has a layering inversion (rex-unit → rex-cli: 2 imports) that mirrors the web package's server→viewer inversion (web-server → web-viewer: 3 imports) — both are small-count reversals in otherwise correct dependency flows, suggesting a systemic pattern of minor boundary leakage at composition roots

The crash zone establishes the clearest example of intentional gateway bypass in the codebase: crash-detector.ts imports shared/view-id.ts directly rather than through external.ts to avoid a cycle. This pattern should be the canonical reference for any future zone facing the same bidirectional-cycle-prevention problem.

boundary-check.test.ts enforces sub-zone governance for crash/ and shared/ but not for viewer-prd-interaction, creating an asymmetric governance surface. The two dual-fragility zones with active production code (crash, viewer-prd-interaction) should have equivalent boundary enforcement in the integration test suite.

use-toast and use-feature-toggle are zero-dependency preact hooks currently stranded in the viewer-prd-interaction dual-fragility zone. Relocating them to web-viewer hub would raise cohesion, lower coupling, and resolve the dual-fragility classification of viewer-prd-interaction without any logic changes — making this the lowest-risk structural improvement available in the web package.

The web package has three distinct test placement strategies: (1) crash zone co-locates tests alongside source in the zone itself, (2) web-integration-tests is a centralized zone for cross-boundary enforcement, (3) web-viewer-unit-tests is an orphan zone caused by tests placed outside both conventions. Standardizing on one strategy — either co-location (crash pattern) or centralized test directories (integration pattern) — would eliminate the orphan zone and prevent future fragmentation.

The boundary-check.test.ts assertion that crash-detector.ts must import ViewId directly from shared/ (not via external.ts) is the only test in the codebase that explicitly validates a gateway bypass. This makes it both a correctness guard and living documentation of the cycle-prevention rationale — a pattern worth replicating for any future intentional gateway bypass.

The monorepo has two structural hubs with analogous topologies but different governance coverage: web-viewer (web package, 300 files, CLAUDE.md governance documented) and rex-prd-engine (rex package, 216 files, no equivalent hub governance policy) — closing this gap would give the rex package the same blast-radius protection the web package has.

The three bidirectional cycles (rex-core↔rex-unit, hench↔hench-guard, web-viewer↔web-server) all share the same root cause: a lower-layer module grew to depend on something in its consumer zone rather than staying strictly downstream — a one-way dependency lint rule enforced in CI would catch new cycles before they merge.

Integration test coverage is asymmetric: cross-package gateway contracts (hench-rex, web-server-viewer) have dedicated tests, but intra-package structural cycles (rex-core↔rex-unit, hench↔hench-guard) do not — the integration zone's scope should be extended to cover intra-package cycle regression as well as cross-package contracts.

The spawn-only architecture (orchestration tier) and the gateway pattern (execution/domain tier) together produce measurable structural benefits visible in the zone metrics: the four zones with zero outbound coupling (root, e2e, integration, web-integration) are all either orchestration-tier entry points or pure test zones — the pattern works and its effect is quantifiable.

The use/viewer-prd-interaction zone, fix/prd-fix-command zone, and rex-core zone are all below the 5-file reliable-metric threshold and all show cohesion ≤ 0.26 — this cluster of low-cohesion micro-zones in the rex and web packages suggests systematic over-fragmentation at the zone boundary; consolidation into their dominant consumer zones (rex-prd-engine and web-viewer respectively) would reduce cross-zone edge count without losing architectural clarity.

The monorepo import graph has a clear enforcement-strength health gradient: spawn-enforced boundaries (orchestration tier) achieve (cohesion 1.0, coupling 0.0); gateway-enforced boundaries (llm-foundation, hench-agent) achieve (≥0.99, ≤0.01); convention-only boundaries (web internal zones) average (~0.6–0.9, 0.1–0.5) — this gradient argues for adding automated boundary tests (analogous to web's boundary-check.test.ts) wherever convention-only enforcement is in use.

The rex package is the only package in the monorepo where the primary domain zone (rex-prd-engine) has outbound imports targeting its own satellite CLI zones — every other package with a hub zone (sourcevision, hench, llm-client) maintains strict one-way dependency flow into the hub; the rex deviation is an anomaly that should be brought into conformance.

rex-prd-engine spans 21 directories and 216 files while its three satellite zones each have 4-5 files — the extreme size asymmetry means small satellite zones cannot form meaningful internal import relationships, producing artificially low cohesion scores (0.18–0.36) that trigger governance alerts on zones that would be healthier absorbed into the engine.

web-viewer participates in 4 bidirectional coupling pairs, sourcevision-analysis-engine in 0, and all other zones in at most 1 — the number of bidirectional pairs a zone participates in is a stronger fragility predictor than cohesion or coupling scores alone

The monorepo's two perfectly cohesive large zones (sourcevision-analysis-engine at 133 files, web-viewer at 300 files with 0.94) take opposite structural positions: sourcevision has zero cross-zone coupling while web-viewer is the most-coupled zone in the codebase — high cohesion does not predict low external coupling

The monorepo's architectural enforcement relies on two non-overlapping layers: TypeScript type checking (compile-time, catches import-shape violations) and e2e file-system tests (runtime, catches structural-layout violations). Neither layer alone is sufficient — a runtime import cycle can satisfy TypeScript while failing architecture-policy.test.js. Both layers must pass in CI to maintain the full contract.

use-toast and use-feature-toggle are general-purpose Preact hooks that currently belong to a dual-fragility zone (viewer-prd-interaction) despite having zero external dependencies. General-purpose hooks with no zone-specific imports should live in the hub zone (web-viewer) where they are closest to their broadest consumer base and subject to stricter boundary governance.

Two structural hubs exist (web-viewer: 300 files, rex-prd-engine: 216 files) but only web-viewer has documented hub governance in CLAUDE.md — rex-prd-engine's equivalent hub topology is undocumented, leaving its blast-radius ungoverned.

Three intra-zone bidirectional cycles (rex-core↔rex-unit, hench↔hench-guard, web-viewer↔web-server) are structurally identical in cause (consumer zone grew a reverse dependency on provider internals) but are addressed individually rather than by a shared prevention rule — a CI import-direction lint check would prevent recurrence across all three simultaneously.

Spawn-only architecture produces zero-coupling test and orchestration zones (root, e2e, integration, web-integration all have 0 outbound coupling) — this is the strongest quantitative evidence that the architectural boundary discipline described in CLAUDE.md is working; these zones serve as a baseline for evaluating future structural changes.

web-viewer (300 files) is the highest blast-radius zone in the monorepo: it receives imports from 5 distinct zones (web-server: 3, web: 23, use: 12, web-unit: 7, crash: 2) and exports to 3 zones (web-server: 39, web: 7, crash: 2) — any API change triggers an 8-zone impact analysis, exceeding even the rex-unit blast radius (which affects 4 zones); strict barrel-only exports for web-viewer are the highest-leverage single governance action available.

web-server → web-viewer: 3 imports contradicts the CLAUDE.md architecture statement that web-server does not import from web-viewer at runtime (viewer is built separately and served as static assets) — these 3 imports are either build-time type references that should be extracted to a shared schema zone, or are runtime imports that represent an active architecture violation requiring immediate remediation.

Three simultaneous bidirectional cycles exist within a single package (rex): engine↔chunked-review, engine↔prd-fix-command, engine↔rex-core-utilities — this density of cycles in one package while all other packages are cycle-free indicates the rex package needs a targeted structural consolidation pass rather than incremental per-zone fixes.

The rex package's zone fragmentation (1 hub + 4 micro-zones) contrasts sharply with the hench package (1 hub + 1 guard zone) despite hench being larger in file count; the difference is that hench has a clear policy boundary (guard) while rex's satellites represent incomplete domain extraction — a consolidation pass merging rex-core-utilities and the domain-logic portions of the satellite zones into rex-prd-engine would bring the rex package to hench-like structural health.

rex-unit (domain layer) imports from rex-cli (CLI layer) in 2 places — this upward dependency inverts the domain→CLI direction and creates a cycle; domain types referenced by CLI commands must not be defined in CLI modules

Three zones contain inverted dependencies that contradict the stated architectural layering (web-server → web-viewer: 3, server-usage-scheduler → web-viewer: 3, rex-unit → rex-cli: 2) — all three are low-count reversals suggesting they are accidental imports that slipped past review rather than intentional design decisions

viewer-prd-interaction is the only dual-fragility production zone in the web package without automated boundary enforcement in boundary-check.test.ts. crash/ and shared/ both have barrel and two-consumer assertions; viewer-prd-interaction has none. This asymmetry means the highest-risk zone has the least automated protection.

Zone IDs have drifted between analysis runs: current IDs (use, rex-core, web, web-server, rex-unit) differ from CLAUDE.md governance documentation names (viewer-prd-interaction, rex-core-utilities, web-dashboard-platform, server-usage-scheduler, rex-prd-engine) — governance rules that reference zones by ID are silently unenforced when zone detection produces different IDs, creating a documentation-reality gap that grows with each analysis run.

CLAUDE.md dual-fragility governance table lists 4 zones but at least 3 additional zones meet or approach criteria: use/viewer-prd-interaction (cohesion 0.26), rex-core (cohesion 0.18), and web (cohesion 0.53 borderline) — the table omissions provide false assurance that governance attention is only needed in 4 places.

Of the four zones in CLAUDE.md dual-fragility table, only web-shared has both documentation-level governance AND automated test enforcement (boundary-check.test.ts two-consumer assertion); the other three (rex-cli, prd-fix-command, crash) have documentation only — violations for these three will not be caught in CI, meaning the table documents risk but does not prevent it.

The monorepo structural health degrades predictably by package: llm-client (0 cycles, perfect metrics), sourcevision (0 cycles), hench (1 low-volume cycle), web (4 bidirectional pairs, convention enforcement), rex (3 cycles in hub, no enforcement) — the degradation tracks directly to the presence or absence of automated boundary tests, not to package size or complexity.

The CLAUDE.md dual-fragility threshold is binary (cohesion < 0.5 AND coupling > 0.5) with no monitoring tier for zones between 0.5 and 0.6 cohesion — web at 0.53 is invisible to the governance rule until it crosses the threshold, at which point the zone already has established coupling patterns that are expensive to reverse; a watch tier at cohesion 0.5–0.65 would provide earlier warning.

The three bidirectional cycles in the rex package can be resolved without any business logic changes: reclassify batch-types.ts into rex-prd-engine, inline core/fix.ts into fix.ts, absorb keywords.ts and verify.ts into rex-prd-engine — all three are file-movement or zone-reclassification operations, confirming the cycles are structural artifacts rather than fundamental design conflicts.

The rex package's satellite zone policy (CLAUDE.md) was defined correctly but not applied retroactively: both batch-types.ts (in analyze/) and core/fix.ts (in core/) predate the CLI-only content rule and violate it, meaning the governance policy has never been in full compliance since it was written.

The rex package's 3 satellite zones contain 13 total files, of which 6 are tests — the production footprint is 7 source files across 3 zones, all of which either violate placement rules, have single consumers, or form return edges into the engine. Consolidating these 7 files would reduce the rex package from 6 zones to 3 and eliminate all active bidirectional cycles.

batch-types.ts, core/fix.ts, keywords.ts, and verify.ts all physically reside in directories belonging to rex-prd-engine (analyze/ and core/) while being zone-classified elsewhere — the rex package has systematic zone-membership/file-location misalignment across 4 files in 3 zones, making the zone map unreliable as a refactoring navigation tool.

Two cross-zone edges cited repeatedly in architecture insights ('web-server → web-viewer: 3' and 'server-usage-scheduler → web-viewer: 3') describe a resolved state — current code inspection finds zero such imports. The sourcevision cross-zone edge data is stale relative to the current codebase; re-running analysis would remove these phantom edges and reduce the reported bidirectional coupling pair count for the web package.

The server-usage-scheduler callback-injection pattern (injecting broadcast, loadPRD, collectAllIds at startup via start.ts) is a distinct decoupling technique from the gateway pattern: gateways control which symbols cross a boundary at import-resolution time; callback injection controls which behavior crosses a boundary at runtime. Both techniques are present in the codebase but only the gateway pattern is documented in CLAUDE.md — the injection seam should be documented to prevent future contributors from replacing it with direct imports.

Zone detection is merging files from src/server/ into the web-viewer zone despite the CLAUDE.md architecture explicitly placing server and viewer in separate zones. This is the root cause of inflated zone file counts (web-viewer reported at 300 files) and overstated server↔viewer coupling metrics. A path-constraint configuration for community detection — anchoring zone membership to directory prefixes for web package zones — would produce metrics that accurately reflect the intended architecture.

The prd-tree directory misclassification (web-dashboard-platform zone, viewer-only consumers) and the server file merging into web-viewer are both zone detection artifacts from the same root cause: Louvain community detection uses import-graph connectivity without path constraints, causing geographically adjacent but architecturally distinct files to be co-zoned. The two artifacts together mean web-dashboard-platform is overcounted (gains prd-tree) and web-viewer is overcounted (gains server files) while web-server is undercounted — all three zone metrics are unreliable until path constraints are applied.

The web package's boundary enforcement has a coverage inversion: boundary-check.test.ts protections are strongest for the healthiest zones (crash cohesion 0.6, web-shared enforced by two-consumer rule) and absent for the zone with the worst metrics (viewer-prd-interaction cohesion 0.26, coupling 0.74). Governance automation strength is inversely correlated with the zones that need it most — a systematic review should add assertions proportional to zone fragility rather than based on historical addition order.

Four independent web package structural issues (crash directory fragmentation, viewer-prd-interaction mixed archetypes, web-viewer-unit-tests orphan zone, missing boundary-check assertions for viewer-prd-interaction) have no dependencies on each other and can be resolved in parallel. Treated as a coordinated batch they reduce the web package's active zone count by two, eliminate all dual-fragility production zones, and close the governance coverage inversion in a single pass — the combined impact significantly exceeds the sum of the individual fixes.

viewer-prd-interaction is the only zone in the codebase that contains both a page-level composition root (prd.ts) and reusable utility modules (use-toast, use-feature-toggle) in the same zone. Pages are route singletons that structurally cannot satisfy the two-consumer rule; utilities must. This archetype mismatch means no consistent governance rule can be applied to the zone as a unit — it is the root cause of why viewer-prd-interaction resists incremental improvement and requires full dissolution rather than targeted extraction.

web-viewer's inbound API contract (api.ts) covers only 3 of its 8 consumer zones — the majority of cross-zone traffic into the monorepo's highest-blast-radius zone flows without a named contract, making the 'barrel-only exports' governance rule structurally unenforceable for web, use, web-unit, and web-server consumers.

The hench ↔ hench-guard bidirectional cycle has a distinct failure mode compared to other cycles in the codebase: because hench (the guarded subject) imports from hench-guard (the policy enforcer), the guard operates as an opt-in library rather than an enforced boundary — the guarded code decides which policy checks to invoke, making the guard semantically weaker than its architectural position implies.

The web-dashboard-platform zone (33 files, cohesion 0.53) is the largest ungoverned production zone in the web package. All other web zones with dual-fragility or high coupling have documented governance policies (web-shared: two-consumer rule; crash: proactive governance; web-server: composition-root rules); the web zone's omission from CLAUDE.md governance is an inconsistency given its size and bidirectional coupling.

Among the five packages, rex is the only one where the package-root zone (rex, 8 files, cohesion 0) has no cohesion — all other package-root zones either have genuine shared entry-point utilities or are small enough (≤2 files) to be unremarkable. The rex zone's persistent cohesion-0 reading creates chronic false positives in structural monitoring that will mask genuine degradation signals if not explicitly excluded or documented.

The monorepo's e2e zone (22 files) has grown to more than 4× the size of the integration zone (5 files) without a proportional increase in documented test priorities. The integration zone's 5 tests are all well-scoped to cross-package seams; the e2e zone's 17 undocumented tests represent unclassified coverage surface that cannot be triaged safely during refactors.

CLAUDE.md dual-fragility governance table covers 4 zones but 3 additional zones (use cohesion 0.26, rex-core cohesion 0.18, web cohesion 0.53 borderline) meet or approach the threshold — the table systematically understates the number of risk zones requiring active addition review.

Zone IDs in current sourcevision analysis (use, rex-core, web, rex-unit) differ from identifiers in CLAUDE.md governance documentation (viewer-prd-interaction, rex-core-utilities, web-dashboard-platform, rex-prd-engine) — governance policies that reference zones by name are decoupled from actual analysis output, making compliance verification impossible to automate.

Three dual-fragility zones in CLAUDE.md governance table (rex-cli, prd-fix-command, crash) have documentation-only governance with no boundary assertions in any test file — violations will not be caught in CI; only web-shared has both documentation and automated enforcement, making it the sole zone where the governance contract is machine-verifiable.

The rex package is the only package where the hub zone (rex-prd-engine, 216 files) has no automated sub-zone boundary test AND simultaneously participates in 3 active bidirectional cycles — the combination of maximum blast radius, maximum cycle count, and zero automated enforcement makes the rex package the highest structural risk in the monorepo.

The rex package has no automated dependency-direction enforcement (no equivalent to web's boundary-check.test.ts) despite having the highest concentration of bidirectional cycles in the monorepo (3 cycles vs 0 for sourcevision and llm-client). A single test asserting that no file under packages/rex/src/ imports from packages/rex/src/cli/ unless it is itself in cli/ would prevent recurrence of the rex-unit → rex-cli upward dependency.

The rex package's zone map cannot be derived from file paths alone: 4 production files reside in directories owned by rex-prd-engine while being classified in satellite zones (batch-types.ts, core/fix.ts, keywords.ts, verify.ts). This physical/logical misalignment means IDE navigation and directory-based tooling will consistently mislead engineers about zone ownership during refactoring.

The web package has a governance coverage inversion: boundary-check.test.ts protections are strongest for zones with healthy metrics and absent for viewer-prd-interaction, the zone with the worst metrics (cohesion 0.26, coupling 0.74). Protection strength should be proportional to zone fragility risk. Adding viewer-prd-interaction boundary assertions to boundary-check.test.ts would make the automated safety net proportional to actual risk distribution.

viewer-prd-interaction mixes a page-level singleton (prd.ts) with reusable hooks and components in a dual-fragility zone. Page components are composition roots by definition — they have exactly one consumer (the router) — making the two-consumer governance rule structurally inapplicable to any zone that contains them. Zones containing page components should be treated as composition roots exempt from the two-consumer rule, or page files should be excluded from shared zones entirely.

The confirmed-phantom 'web-viewer → web-server: 39' edge means the cross-zone import list driving all previous architectural governance discussions contains at least one completely fabricated data point. The prior insight noting this edge was 'potentially resolved' understated the issue — it is not a resolved edge but a zone-boundary misclassification artifact from a stale analysis run. Any governance decision that prioritized this edge over real structural issues has been misdirected.

The monorepo contains two distinct classes of cohesion-0 zones: (1) test/config orphans created by proximity-grouping without import edges (web-unit, rex-e2e-config), and (2) structural artifacts from zone rename churn (fix→prd-fix-command). Both classes share cohesion 0 but require different remediation — orphan zones need consolidation or test co-location; rename artifacts resolve themselves after re-analysis. Treating both as structural risk zones rather than detection artifacts misdirects governance effort.

Dynamic imports (await import()) are a blind spot in the current import graph analysis: analyze.ts has 4 dynamic import call sites to chunked-review.ts that are not captured as cross-zone edges in sourcevision's static analysis. If other production files use dynamic imports across zone boundaries, the reported coupling scores for those zones are systematically undercounted. A codebase-wide grep for dynamic import patterns across zone boundaries would reveal the full extent of this undercounting.

The rex package has a zone-membership/physical-directory mismatch that is more severe than previously characterized: analyze.ts and chunked-review.ts are in the SAME directory (packages/rex/src/cli/commands/) but are classified into different zones (rex-prd-engine and chunked-review respectively). Previous insights noted mismatch where zone boundaries crossed directories; this case has two zone boundaries within a single directory, making the zone map entirely useless as a navigation aid for that directory.

The three perfectly-cohesive large zones (monorepo-orchestration 1.0, llm-foundation 1.0, sourcevision 1.0) achieve their scores through mechanisms that are fragile in different ways: spawn-only (binary — one static import breaks it), package.json exports with gateway mediation (degrades if consumers add a wildcard path import), and internal zone self-containment (degrades if the zone grows a dependency on a sibling package). Each mechanism needs a different type of CI enforcement to remain reliable.

The cross-zone import data shows web-viewer → crash: 2 as the only edge between the zones, with no crash → web-viewer edge in the current data — web-viewer imports from crash/index.ts (crash-recovery-banner, use-crash-recovery), and crash/crash-detector.ts imports shared/view-id.ts directly (documented gateway bypass). The bidirectional coupling cited in CLAUDE.md exists but runs crash→shared (not crash→web-viewer); the governance table entry describing crash as 'bidirectional with web-viewer' should be revised to clarify the actual direction: web-viewer imports crash, crash imports web-shared directly — a unidirectional viewer→crash dependency with a separate crash→shared bypass, not a mutual cycle.

The rex CLI contains 30+ command files across a flat directory (packages/rex/src/cli/commands/) including decomposition-review.ts, smart-add.ts, smart-add-duplicates.ts, reorganize.ts, reshape.ts, health.ts, report.ts, sync.ts, move.ts, token-format.ts, usage.ts — none of these appear in any named zone, meaning the bulk of the CLI surface is zone-invisible. The current zone map only captures 3 of the 30+ CLI files in satellite zones; the remaining 27 files form an ungoverned zone fragment that is larger than any documented satellite zone.

loe-display-chunked-review.test.ts and loe-display-proposal-tree.test.ts share a 'loe-display' naming prefix but are classified in different zones (chunked-review and rex-prd-engine respectively), with format-loe.ts as the apparent source for the proposal-tree variant — the LOE display subsystem is split across two zones and two test files with no documentation of the intentional split or the shared naming convention.

The two 2-file zones in the monorepo (web-unit: cohesion 0, web-landing: cohesion 1) produce opposite extreme cohesion scores based solely on whether their two files import each other — at 2 files, any pair with no mutual import scores 0 and any pair with one mutual import scores 1. The 5-file reliable-metric threshold already documented in prior insights understates the actual floor: 2-file zones should be treated as metric-free and governance decisions should be suspended until they reach at least 3 files with at least one mutual import.

The monorepo has no shared intra-package dependency-direction enforcement layer: each package independently implements (or omits) boundary tests. Web has boundary-check.test.ts; rex and hench have none despite having active bidirectional cycles. A monorepo-level test file that enforces one-way dependency rules across all packages (analogous to how architecture-policy.test.js enforces spawn-only at the orchestration tier) would standardize prevention without duplicating per-package infrastructure.

The three perfectly isolated zones (sourcevision-analysis-engine, landing-page, llm-client) achieve isolation through three distinct mechanisms: (1) gateway enforcement at the single entry point (sourcevision), (2) build-entry separation — a separate HTML bundle entry point (landing-page), (3) foundation-tier positioning with no upward consumers (llm-client). Each mechanism operates at a different layer (import graph, bundler, architectural tier) and provides different guarantees. Documenting these as three named isolation strategies would help architects select the appropriate mechanism for new zones requiring strong boundary guarantees.

The normalized coupling percentage is inversely proportional to zone size, making it an unreliable cross-zone comparator: web-viewer at coupling 0.06 with 7 active cross-zone edges is more structurally complex than web-dashboard-platform at coupling 0.47 with 5 edges, yet appears healthier by the percentage metric. Governance tooling should surface raw cross-zone edge counts alongside normalized coupling scores, with a flag that triggers when edge count exceeds a threshold (e.g., 5) regardless of the coupling percentage.

The monorepo has two structurally analogous pairs that are governed asymmetrically: (web-server + web-viewer) and (rex-prd-engine + rex-cli). In both pairs, the lower-level zone (web-server, rex-prd-engine) has a reverse dependency on the upper zone creating a cycle, the primary zone is large (300 and 216 files), and the cycle is low-volume (3 and 2 imports). Despite this structural symmetry, web has documented hub governance and an active boundary test while rex has neither — the governance gap for rex is not incremental but an exact mirror of the web package's most-governed zone.

The callback-injection decoupling pattern (server-usage-scheduler) and the gateway module pattern (rex-gateway.ts, domain-gateway.ts) are both present in the codebase but serve different coupling dimensions: gateways control static import coupling (visible to zone analysis), callback injection controls runtime behavioral coupling (invisible to zone analysis). The monorepo has no tooling or documentation that captures runtime behavioral coupling seams — a conventions document listing all injection seams alongside gateway modules would complete the boundary documentation.

Five zones have cohesion scores that fall into three bands: perfect (1.0: sourcevision-analysis-engine, landing-page), high (0.87–0.94: server-usage-scheduler, web-viewer), and moderate (0.53: web-dashboard-platform). The moderate band zone (web-dashboard-platform) is the only zone in this batch contaminated by build infrastructure files — removing build.js and dev.js from zone analysis would likely push it into the high band, making build-file exclusion the single highest-leverage configuration change for improving metric accuracy in this batch.

The monorepo has an implicit zone size threshold below which all cohesion scores are unreliable (documented as 5 files in CLAUDE.md for web-shared). However, this threshold is applied inconsistently: web-shared (3 files) is explicitly flagged as below threshold while landing-page (2 files), rex-core (4 files), and web-unit (2 files) are analyzed and cited in governance decisions without the threshold caveat. A global rule that suppresses cohesion-based governance alerts for zones with fewer than 5 production files would eliminate false positives from three zones simultaneously.

tests/helpers/ is a singleton directory with exactly one file (crash-detector-test-support.ts). Singleton directories impose directory-traversal overhead without structural benefit. The monorepo has no other single-file helper directories — this is an outlier that should be resolved by co-locating the file with its sole consumer.

The hooks/ directory (packages/web/src/viewer/hooks/, 29 files) is the largest unharnessed import surface in the web viewer: 29 files imported by direct leaf path with no barrel, no boundary assertion, and no named public contract. This is structurally inconsistent with crash/ (barrel enforced by boundary-check.test.ts) and shared/ (barrel enforced by boundary-check.test.ts + two-consumer assertion). A hooks/index.ts barrel with a corresponding boundary-check assertion would close the largest enforcement gap in the web package.

build-output-contract.test.ts covers browser-side build artifacts (viewer, landing) but not server-side artifacts. The web package has a dual build (browser + server), making the build contract asymmetric: browser regressions are caught in CI, server build regressions are invisible until a runtime deployment failure.

The implicit two-layer CLI contract testing strategy (full public surface via cli-arg-contracts + orchestration-spawn subset via cli-contract) is effective but undocumented. Without documentation, the strategy is vulnerable to consolidation by a future maintainer who perceives the two files as redundant coverage of the same concern.

Moving crash-recovery-banner.ts into crash/, moving accessibility.test.ts and status-filter.test.ts into tests/unit/viewer/, and dissolving viewer-prd-interaction into web-viewer together reduce the web package's active zone count from ~8 to ~5. The reduction also eliminates 5+ synthetic cross-zone edges that currently inflate governance alert counts and distort bidirectional coupling metrics for web-viewer — a metric hygiene benefit that compounds with each subsequent analysis run.

use-toast and use-feature-toggle violate the two-consumer rule today: each has exactly one consumer (prd.ts). This means they do not qualify for placement in a shared zone under the current governance rules. The correct resolution is either: (a) keep them in viewer-prd-interaction until a second consumer zone emerges, or (b) classify prd.ts as a composition root exempt from the two-consumer rule and treat the co-located hooks as composition-root-local utilities rather than shared modules.

The web package has three test placement patterns (crash zone co-location, centralized unit tests in tests/unit/viewer/, centralized integration tests in tests/integration/) but only the integration pattern has a named zone in sourcevision output. This asymmetry makes the co-located crash tests invisible at the zone level — they appear as crash zone production files rather than as a distinct test zone, potentially confusing zone metric calculations for crash cohesion.

TESTING.md and CLAUDE.md both maintain independent 'Required Tests' tables listing the same two entries (cli-dev.test.js, scheduler-startup.test.js). Dual-source documentation for the same fact creates a silent divergence risk: a newly required test added to one file but not the other would go undetected. A single source of truth (TESTING.md only, referenced from CLAUDE.md) with a CI assertion scanning for the 'REQUIRED TEST' annotation would eliminate the duplication.

The integration growth policy in TESTING.md (integration file count >= 15% of e2e file count) uses file count rather than test case count as its metric. At current scale (5 integration vs 20 e2e = 25%), the policy is satisfied. As e2e grows with new CLI commands, the ratio will decline and can be satisfied by adding minimal single-test-case files. Augmenting the policy with a minimum test case count per seam (e.g., at least 2 test cases per documented architectural boundary) would prevent shallow compliance.

gateway-pipeline.test.js references '@see TESTING.md — gateway admission criterion' in its JSDoc, but hench-rex-gateway-pipeline.test.js (added later to the same seam) omits this cross-reference. The admission criterion applies equally to both files; the inconsistent JSDoc means engineers who discover hench-rex-gateway-pipeline.test.js first will not find the formal admission criterion without a separate TESTING.md search.

tests/integration/README.md explains the .js extension convention and the build-time vs compile-time alias resolution rationale, but TESTING.md's 'Integration Test Tier' section does not link to or mention README.md. Engineers reading TESTING.md as the canonical testing reference may miss the build-boundary rationale, leading to accidental .ts file creation that silently bypasses the dist/-only contract.

sourcevision's static import graph analysis cannot detect dynamic imports (await import(), require() with variable paths, or import() in conditional branches). The confirmed 4 dynamic import sites in analyze.ts→chunked-review are invisible to zone coupling metrics. A supplementary CI check scanning for cross-zone dynamic imports would close this systematic enforcement blind spot before it grows.

The current zone analysis data contains at least one confirmed phantom edge (web-viewer → web-server: 39) that has been driving architectural governance discussions for multiple analysis passes. Before the next governance pass, re-run sourcevision analysis to produce a fresh zone map. All architectural priorities derived from stale cross-zone edge counts should be treated as provisional until confirmed by fresh analysis.

Both rex-core-utilities (0 internal imports between 2 production files) and rex-e2e-config (0 internal imports between 8 files) have zero production cohesion. Add an explicit rule to zone governance: any zone where no two production files share an import edge is a detection artifact, not an architectural zone. Documenting this classification criterion would allow automated filtering of artifact zones from governance dashboards.

The llm-foundation './dist/*' wildcard export and the absence of sourcevision entry point declarations together mean any file in the foundation tier can be imported directly by any intra-monorepo consumer without triggering a lint, type, or boundary test warning. The foundation tier is the most critical boundary in the four-tier hierarchy — closing this gap requires either removing the wildcard export (breaking change) or adding zone-level entry point assertions to sourcevision configuration (non-breaking).

The rex CLI flat directory (src/cli/commands/) contains 30+ command files but only 3 are captured in named satellite zones — the other 27+ command files are zone-invisible, making the rex package's zone map unreliable as a structural navigation tool. Zone detection should be re-run with the full rex CLI directory in scope, or a catch-all 'rex-cli-commands' zone should be explicitly defined to surface the ungoverned CLI surface.

CLAUDE.md describes crash as having 'bidirectional coupling with web-viewer' but current imports show only web-viewer → crash (viewer imports crash's recovery utilities) with crash importing web-shared directly (documented bypass) rather than web-viewer — update the governance table to accurately describe the actual dependency direction to prevent engineers from applying the wrong mitigation strategy.

Neither the rex package nor the hench package has an intra-package one-way dependency direction test despite having 4 active bidirectional cycles between them (rex: 3, hench: 1) — adding a single assertion per package that no file in src/ imports from a higher-tier directory unless it is itself in that tier would provide the same CI regression protection that web's boundary-check.test.ts provides for the web package.

The hench-agent governance section in CLAUDE.md specifies barrel re-export rules for cross-sub-zone imports but does not reference any enforcing test — add a boundary assertion to hench's test suite verifying cross-sub-zone imports flow through barrel index.ts files to activate the documented governance policy before the zone reaches web-viewer's scale.

Add a build-file exclusion list to sourcevision configuration: packages/web/build.js, packages/web/dev.js, and any future *.config.js or build-tooling files should be excluded from zone membership analysis. These files share directory proximity with runtime components but have no runtime import relationships with them — their inclusion corrupts cohesion and coupling metrics for any zone they land in.

Implement a zone-size gate in governance tooling: suppress cohesion-based dual-fragility alerts for zones with fewer than 5 production (non-test) files. Apply this gate retrospectively to landing-page (2 files), web-unit (2 files), and rex-core (4 files) to eliminate the three most persistent false-positive governance signals in current analysis output.

Surface raw cross-zone edge count as a first-class metric alongside normalized coupling percentage in zone reports. Enforce a 'high-edge-count watch' flag when a zone has more than 5 distinct cross-zone edges regardless of coupling percentage — this would flag web-viewer (7 edges) while correctly leaving sourcevision-analysis-engine (0 edges) ungoverned.

Document the three isolation mechanisms present in the monorepo as named strategies in CLAUDE.md: 'Gateway isolation' (single import entry point, e.g., sourcevision-analysis-engine), 'Build-entry isolation' (separate bundler entry point, e.g., landing-page), and 'Tier isolation' (foundation-layer positioning with no upward consumers, e.g., llm-client). New zones requiring strong isolation guarantees should select a named strategy rather than relying on convention.

Create a monorepo-level 'injection seam registry' alongside the gateway module table in CLAUDE.md. Seams that use callback injection (start.ts → register-scheduler.ts) rather than gateway imports are invisible to static analysis — listing them explicitly with their injected interface types prevents future contributors from inadvertently replacing injection with direct imports to 'simplify' the code.

Add a hooks/index.ts barrel to packages/web/src/viewer/hooks/ and a boundary-check.test.ts assertion requiring consumers to import through the barrel. The hooks directory is the largest leaf-import surface in the web viewer (29 files) with no enforcement — analogous to the pre-barrel state of crash/ and shared/ before their boundary assertions were added.

Document the two-layer CLI contract testing strategy in CLAUDE.md: cli-arg-contracts.test.js guards the full public CLI surface; cli-contract.test.js guards the orchestration-spawn boundary. Without this documentation, future reviewers may consolidate the two files, silently eliminating one enforcement layer.

Extend build-output-contract.test.ts to cover server bundle artifacts (dist/server/ or equivalent). The current contract is browser-only (viewer + landing), leaving server build regressions undetected in CI until a deployment failure.

Eliminate tests/helpers/ by moving crash-detector-test-support.ts into packages/web/tests/unit/viewer/ alongside crash-detector.test.ts. The helpers/ directory exists for a single file with a single consumer — an unnecessary indirection that does not meet the bar for a shared directory.

Consolidate the 'Required Tests' table into TESTING.md as the single source of truth. Replace the corresponding CLAUDE.md table entry with a reference link to TESTING.md. Add an architecture-policy test assertion that scans required test file paths for the 'REQUIRED TEST' annotation string, making the required-test contract machine-verifiable in CI rather than documentation-only.

Augment the integration test growth policy in TESTING.md and integration-coverage-policy.test.js to enforce a minimum test-case-per-boundary count (not just file count). The current file-count ratio can be gamed by adding empty skeleton files; a per-seam minimum (e.g., at least 2 test cases per documented architectural boundary) would enforce substantive coverage growth.

Add a cross-reference in hench-rex-gateway-pipeline.test.js JSDoc to '@see TESTING.md — gateway admission criterion', matching the pattern established in gateway-pipeline.test.js. keywords.ts and verify.ts share no internal imports and exist as a zone solely because they both import from and are imported by rex-prd-engine — absorbing both files directly into rex-prd-engine would eliminate the zone, break the bidirectional cycle, and improve the engine's self-contained cohesion without adding external coupling.

Three of the four zones that depend on rex-prd-engine (chunked-review, prd-fix-command, rex-core-utilities) each produce a return edge back into the engine, forming a systemic multi-cycle pattern rather than isolated incidents — a single structural fix (strictly enforcing one-way dependency into the engine) would resolve all three cycles simultaneously.

web-server-viewer-boundary test should assert an exhaustive allowlist of permitted web-server→web-viewer imports (currently 3), not just the absence of a known bad import — without an allowlist, new reverse imports silently pass.

viewer-prd-interaction contains structurally incompatible file archetypes: a page-level route component (prd.ts, singleton by definition) and reusable utilities (use-toast, use-feature-toggle, bulk-actions.ts). boundary-check.test.ts provides barrel and two-consumer assertions for crash/ and shared/ but has zero corresponding assertions for viewer-prd-interaction (cohesion 0.26, coupling 0.74) — the only other dual-fragility production zone in the web package.
